perturbation.original_text: MusclePharm shall, throughout the Term of the Agreement and for a period of not less than four years thereafter, obtain and maintain at its own cost and expense from a qualified insurance company licensed to do business in California and New York, a commercial general liability insurance policy including coverage for contractual liability (applying to the terms and conditions of this agreement), product liability, personal injury liability, and advertiser's liability, in a form approved by the AS Parties, in the amount of at least Five Million Dollars (US$5,000,000) per occurrence naming the AS Parties (for the avoidance of doubt, specifically including each of Lender, Endorser, and Fitness) as additional named insureds. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, such policy shall provide protection against any and all claims, demands, and causes of action arising out of any defects or failure to perform, alleged or otherwise, of the Products and Licensed Products or any material used in connection therewith or any use thereof. The policy shall provide for ten (10) days notice to the AS Parties from the insurer by Registered or Certified Mail, return receipt requested, in the event of any modification, cancellation, or termination thereof. MusclePharm agrees to furnish the AS Parties a certificate of insurance evidencing same within thirty (30) days after execution of this Agreement and, in no event, shall MusclePharm manufacture, distribute, advertise, or sell the Licensed Products prior to receipt by the AS Parties of such evidence of insurance. MusclePharm shall be responsible to provide for any appearances pursuant to this Agreement by Endorser appropriate certificates of insurance with coverage limits of at least Five Million Dollars (US$5,000,000) per occurrence endorsed to name the AS Parties as additional named insureds with respect to claims arising out of appearances by Endorser. MusclePharm shall be responsible to pay the deductible under any such insurance policies with respect to any claims made under such policies.
perturbation.changed_text: MusclePharm shall obtain and maintain at its own cost and expense a commercial general liability insurance policy. MusclePharm agrees to furnish the AS Parties a certificate of insurance evidencing same within thirty (30) days after execution of this Agreement. MusclePharm shall be responsible to pay the deductible under any such insurance policies with respect to any claims made under such policies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the detailed insurance requirements, including the minimum coverage amount, specific types of liability coverage, requirement for the insurance company to be licensed in California and New York, and the four-year tail coverage and required notification to the AS Parties, the modified text creates a legal risk for the AS Parties. This is because California law sets specific requirements for insurance policies, and this omission prevents the AS Parties from ensuring adequate protection. These minimum coverage requirements are often tied to the type of business and potential liabilities involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 11580.2 outlines specific requirements for uninsured motorist coverage in insurance policies. By removing the clause detailing the specifics of the insurance policy MusclePharm is required to maintain, the modified text could allow MusclePharm to obtain a policy that doesn't meet these minimum requirements, thus leaving the AS Parties vulnerable and without legally mandated protections in case of a claim.

CLASSES OF INSURANCE [1880 - 12880.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 3. LIABILITY, WORKERS' COMPENSATION, AND COMMON CARRIER LIABILITY INSURANCE [11550 - 11895] ( Heading of Part 3 amended by Stats. 1979, Ch. 373. ) CHAPTER 1. General Regulations [11550 - 11629.88] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) ARTICLE 2. Actions on Policies Containing Liability Provisions [11580 - 11589.5] ( Article 2 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) 11580.2. (a) (1) No policy of bodily injury liability insurance covering liability arising out of the ownership, maintenance, or use of any motor vehicle, except for policies that provide insurance in the Republic of Mexico issued or delivered in this state by nonadmitted Mexican insurers, shall be issued or delivered in this state to the owner or operator of a motor vehicle, or shall be issued or delivered by any insurer licensed in this state upon any motor vehicle then principally used or principally garaged in this state, unless the policy contains, or has added to it by endorsement, a provision with coverage limits at least equal to the limits specified in subdivision (m) and in no case less than the financial responsibility requirements specified in Section 16056 of the Veh
perturbation.justification: The original contract text stipulated that MusclePharm obtain and maintain a commercial general liability insurance policy with specific coverage details, including a minimum coverage amount of $5,000,000, coverage for contractual liability, product liability, personal injury liability, and advertiser's liability, and that the insurance company be licensed in California and New York. It also specified a four-year tail coverage and notification requirements. The modified text removes all these specific insurance requirements, only stating that 'MusclePharm shall obtain and maintain at its own cost and expense a commercial general liability insurance policy.' This omission creates a legal risk. The Law Explanation highlights that California Insurance Code § 11580.2 outlines specific requirements for uninsured motorist coverage, and by removing the clause detailing the specifics of the insurance policy, the modified text could allow MusclePharm to obtain a policy that doesn't meet these minimum requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Insurance Code - INS DIVISION 2, also supports this, indicating that liability insurance policies issued in California must contain provisions with coverage limits at least equal to specific limits. Therefore, the omission of the detailed insurance requirements in the modified text creates a legal contradiction because it potentially allows for non-compliance with California insurance regulations, thus undermining legally mandated protections. The modified text removes core legal obligations around insurance coverage that were previously clearly defined.

perturbation.original_text: MusclePharm and the manufacturers will, at a minimum, materially comply with all applicable wage and hour laws, rules, regulations, and industry standards. MusclePharm and the manufacturers agree that, where local industry standards are higher than applicable legal requirements, MusclePharm and manufacturer will meet the higher local standards.
perturbation.changed_text: MusclePharm and the manufacturers will comply with all applicable wage and hour laws, rules, regulations, and industry standards.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the part of the sentence that states "MusclePharm and the manufacturers agree that, where local industry standards are higher than applicable legal requirements, MusclePharm and manufacturer will meet the higher local standards," this introduces a legal risk. This is because California law dictates that employers must adhere to the higher of the state or federal standard in regard to minimum wage, overtime and other protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: CA Labor Code § 1197 establishes the minimum wage for employees in California. Removing the requirement to comply with the *higher* of local or legal standards could lead to MusclePharm paying employees less than the state-mandated minimum wage, violating California law. This modified text now implies that MusclePharm only needs to meet the bare minimum legal requirements, regardless of whether local industry standards are higher, contradicting the CA Labor Code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 1197. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 2. EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 4. EMPLOYEES [1171 - 1413] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 1122. ) CHAPTER 1. Wages, Hours and Working Conditions [1171 - 1207] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 1197. The minimum wage for employees fixed by the commission or by any applicable state or local law, is the minimum wage to be paid to employees, and the payment of a lower wage than the minimum so fixed is unlawful. This section does not change the applicability of local minimum wage laws to any entity. (Amended by Stats. 2015, Ch. 783, Sec. 2. (AB 970) Effective January 1, 2016.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit requirement to adhere to higher local industry standards where they exceed legal requirements. This directly contradicts CA Labor Code § 1197, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, establishes that the minimum wage fixed by the commission or any applicable state or local law is the minimum wage to be paid to employees, making any lower wage unlawful. By omitting the part of the original text that required adherence to these higher local standards, the modified contract creates a scenario where MusclePharm could potentially pay employees less than what is legally required under local industry standards, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Licensed Products shall be of the highest quality and manufactured, produced, sold, distributed and promoted in strict compliance with all applicable laws and regulations, and be of substantially the same or better quality as the samples previously submitted by MusclePharm. MusclePharm shall be responsible for ensuring that the products are properly designed and manufactured for safe use and shall promptly and fairly address and resolve all consumer complaints and warranty claims. MusclePharm hereby acknowledges that the AS Parties are not competent to determine whether the products are safe for sale and/or distribution to the public at large. Accordingly, the AS Parties' approval rights relate to aspects of quality and not to a determination of the safety of the products and any approvals given by the AS Parties of the products shall in no way detract from the MusclePharm's obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: The Licensed Products shall be manufactured, produced, sold, distributed and promoted in compliance with applicable laws and regulations. MusclePharm shall be responsible for addressing consumer complaints.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'highest quality,' 'strict compliance,' the obligation to ensure 'safe use,' and the 'promptly and fairly' handling of consumer claims, MusclePharm reduces its accountability regarding product safety and quality under California law. By removing the important part that MusclePharm shall be responsible for ensuring that the products are properly designed and manufactured for safe use, MusclePharm is reducing accountability regarding product safety and quality under California law. This specifically contradicts consumer protection laws that require businesses to ensure product safety and provide adequate remedies for defective products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 1750
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in transactions intended to result or which result in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer. By weakening the language related to quality, safety, and claim resolution, the modified clause creates a risk that MusclePharm could engage in practices that violate the CLRA, such as selling products that do not meet reasonable consumer expectations or failing to address complaints adequately. This contradicts the CLRA's intent to protect consumers from deceptive business practices.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens MusclePharm's responsibility regarding product safety and consumer protection. The original text mandates 'highest quality,' 'strict compliance,' ensuring 'safe use,' and 'promptly and fairly' handling consumer claims. The revised text eliminates these specific obligations, only requiring compliance with 'applicable laws and regulations' and 'addressing consumer complaints' without specifying the standard of care or quality. The scraped law snippet 1, citing California Civil Code 1750, introduces the Consumers Legal Remedies Act, which aims to protect consumers from unfair or deceptive practices. By removing the specific obligations related to quality and safety, the revised contract increases the risk that MusclePharm could engage in practices that violate the CLRA, thus creating a contradiction, as the omission weakens consumer protections that the CLRA aims to provide.
